answers.field_label: Other Goal Describe
answers.value:
We have an additional school-based wellness goal focused on integrating social-emotional learning (SEL) with physical wellness by creating "Mindful Movement" breaks in classrooms and offering staff wellness challenges.

The SEL component emphasizes teaching students basic stress-management techniques—such as deep breathing, chair yoga, and short guided mindfulness activities—embedded into the school day, not only during PE or health class. Classroom teachers are encouraged (but not yet required) to incorporate at least one 3–5 minute mindful movement break in the morning and one in the afternoon. These breaks are designed to be inclusive for students with physical limitations and can be done standing or seated.

The staff wellness challenge portion of this goal recognizes that students model much of their behavior after adults in the building. Each semester we run a voluntary wellness challenge for staff (teachers, paraprofessionals, office staff, custodians, and cafeteria staff). The focus areas rotate between increasing daily steps, improving hydration, prioritizing sleep, and managing stress. We track participation through simple self-report logs and optional use of pedometers or fitness apps. Small incentives (e.g., water bottles, yoga mats, or a healthy breakfast bar in the staff lounge) are provided using donated funds and local wellness grants.

We are working toward formalizing this goal in our wellness policy so that it is sustained over time rather than depending on one or two enthusiastic staff members. We also want to connect these efforts with family engagement by sharing simple SEL and movement activities families can try at home, so that wellness is reinforced beyond the school day.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Notes On Goals For Other School Based Activities
answers.value:
Persona and context: This assessment is being completed by me, Sarah Martinez, Wellness Committee Chair and Assistant Principal at Riverside Middle School, a public 6–8 school in a mid-sized district. We serve approximately 720 students, with a diverse population (about 48% eligible for free or reduced-price meals). Our district updated its Local School Wellness Policy in 2022 to align with current USDA requirements and state guidelines. Below are detailed notes on how our written policy translates into daily practice in our building.

Free drinking water: We have explicit language in our district wellness policy stating that free drinking water must be available during meal periods and throughout the school day. In practice, this is fully in place. We have working water fountains in each hallway, two bottle-filling stations (installed in 2021 near the cafeteria and gym), and a water cooler in the cafeteria with disposable cups for students who forget their water bottles. Custodial staff check fountains daily. When we had an issue with a fountain on the 3rd floor last fall, it was repaired within 48 hours. During lunch, students can access water from the bottle-filling station or the cooler without asking permission.

School nutrition staff qualifications and continuing education: Our policy requires that all school nutrition staff meet local HR hiring criteria and USDA professional standards. All current staff, including our food service manager, meet those standards—this is fully implemented. For continuing education, the written policy mirrors federal requirements (minimum annual training hours). Implementation is partially in place because while the manager and full-time staff consistently complete their required hours through the state education department webinars and in-person trainings, some of our part-time staff have had difficulty scheduling training time around second jobs and caregiving responsibilities. We are working with the district food service director to offer more short, on-site micro-trainings during prep time and early-release days.

Meal environment, space, and time to eat: The policy clearly states that students should have a safe, clean, and pleasant environment for meals and sufficient time to eat. Safety and cleanliness are fully in place: custodians clean tables between lunch periods, trash is removed promptly, and we have clear procedures for managing student movement and behavior in the cafeteria. However, our cafeteria space is tight for our current enrollment, and we run four lunch periods between 10:45 a.m. and 1:15 p.m. Students technically receive a 25-minute lunch period, but by the time they get through the line, some only have 12–15 minutes to actually eat, especially on days with more popular menu items. This is why we rated implementation of adequate space and time as only partially in place.

Alternate breakfast models: The wellness policy allows for alternate breakfast service models (grab-and-go carts, breakfast in the classroom, and second-chance breakfast). At Riverside, we currently use a grab-and-go breakfast cart stationed in the main lobby from 7:30–7:55 a.m. and also serve traditional cafeteria breakfast. Participation increased when we added the cart, but we have not yet implemented second-chance breakfast for students who arrive late or aren’t hungry first thing in the morning. Teachers have also expressed concerns about trash and spills if we move toward breakfast in the classroom. Because we use only one of the three possible models, we marked this as partially in place.

Hand washing and sanitizing: The district policy states that students must have access to handwashing or hand sanitizer before meals. In practice, this is fully implemented: each grade-level hallway has a restroom students can use before lunch, and we installed hand sanitizer dispensers at both cafeteria entrances. Our advisory teachers also build in a quick hand-sanitizing routine before they escort students to lunch, which has become a habitual part of our daily schedule.

Access to food service areas: The policy restricts access to kitchens and storage areas to authorized staff only. This is fully in place. Kitchen doors remain locked when not in use, and students are never allowed into food preparation or storage areas. Maintenance and custodial staff only enter when accompanied by food service staff or with explicit permission.

Nutrition information and menu feedback: Our policy states that families and staff should have access to nutrition information for school meals and that students and parents should be included in menu planning via surveys and taste tests. Implementation is partial. The district posts monthly menus and basic nutrition info (calories, major allergens) on the district website, and we occasionally share highlights in our school newsletter. However, the information is not always user-friendly for families with limited English proficiency or limited internet access. We have held two student menu-tasting events in the last two years (for example, sampling a new roasted chickpea snack and a whole-grain breakfast bar), but we do not yet have a consistent, scheduled process for student and parent input. We hope to conduct an annual online and paper survey in both English and Spanish.

Outside funding and programs: The written policy encourages schools to seek grants and partnerships to support wellness. We are partially implementing this. Over the last three years, we have successfully secured a small grant from a local hospital to install one of our water bottle-filling stations and have partnered with the county health department to provide a "Rethink Your Drink" campaign. However, we have not yet systematically mapped out all potential external resources, and many opportunities are discovered informally rather than through a planned strategy. Our goal is to designate one Wellness Committee member to focus on grants and partnerships.

Staff training on wellness policy: Our policy requires that all staff be informed about the wellness policy. In reality, this is done partially. At the start of the year, we include a 20-minute overview of the wellness policy during our August in-service day and send staff a digital copy via email. New staff receive the policy as part of their onboarding packet. However, there is limited follow-up training, and we have not yet provided role-specific examples (e.g., how classroom teachers can support nutrition education, or how coaches can model healthy hydration and snack choices). We are planning to add short refresher sessions during faculty meetings and recorded video modules staff can watch on their own time.

School-based activities and fundraising: The policy states that school-based activities (such as field days, assemblies, and family nights) and fundraising projects should align with wellness goals and avoid promoting unhealthy foods. Implementation is partially in place. For example, our annual Field Day includes water stations, fruit snacks, and active games rather than candy prizes. Our PTA has shifted from candy sales to a fun run and a read-a-thon. However, some clubs still default to selling candy bars or sugary baked goods as fundraisers, especially when they are under time pressure to raise money. We have started providing a list of alternative fundraising ideas (such as plant sales, school-branded merchandise, and car washes), but the transition is still ongoing.

Role modeling and communication with families: The wellness policy encourages adults in the school community to model healthy behaviors and requires communication with parents about nutrition and physical activity. We see partial implementation here. Some teachers and coaches visibly model wellness by drinking water, taking walking meetings, and talking with students about balanced eating. Others still regularly bring large sugary drinks into class. We send wellness tips in our monthly family newsletter (in English and Spanish) and post about wellness events on our school’s social media accounts, but we know not all families receive or read these messages. We are exploring the use of text-message reminders and short bilingual videos to increase engagement.

Indoor air quality: Our district has a separate Healthy Learning Environment policy that is referenced in the wellness policy. Indoor air quality is monitored through regular HVAC maintenance, filter changes, and prompt response to reports of odors or moisture. We have had occasional concerns about humidity in a few older classrooms, but maintenance addressed these by adjusting ventilation and using dehumidifiers during the summer. Because there is a clear system and consistent follow-through, we rated this as fully in place.

Additional SEL and staff wellness goal: As described above, we are informally implementing mindful movement and staff wellness challenges, but these are not yet fully codified in the written policy. Participation in staff challenges is about 40–50% of staff each semester, and classroom implementation of mindful movement breaks varies widely by teacher. Our next steps include adding explicit language about SEL-integrated wellness to the policy, offering simple scripted activities for teachers, and collecting basic data (e.g., how many classrooms are using movement breaks at least three times per week) so we can monitor progress over time.
